data.title: USDA moves to pull soda and candy from food stamps - report
data.description:
As part of Washington’s Make America Healthy Again initiative, the U.S. Department of Agriculture is fast-tracking states’ requests to remove soda and candy from the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (“SNAP”).

Breaking with a decades-long policy by the USDA to reject efforts to restrict certain foods from food stamps, Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins is asking state governors to propose pilot programs that would limit what SNAP benefits can be used for.

Arkansas governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders took up the challenge and is proposing a five-year restriction on soda (diet and regular), candy, and vegetable and fruit drinks with less than 50% natural juice under the state’s food stamps program, challenging intense lobbying efforts by Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO), PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP), and Keurig Dr. Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP).

"Our goal is not to hurt any industry, but to support and promote healthier lifestyles," Sanders said in an interview with the Wall Street Journal. "No one is saying you can't have a Diet Coke or a candy bar. We're saying you can't do it with taxpayer money.”

Under current federal SNAP eligibility, benefits can be used to purchase any food or food product intended for human consumption, except for tobacco, alcohol beverages, and prepared hot foods. While eligibility varies by state, most states allow food stamps to be used for “junk foods” to include soft drinks and candy.

If approved, new SNAP eligibility guidelines would be in effect by summer 2026.

data.title: Freightos estimates 25% higher transactions in Q1
data.description:
Freight booking and payment platform Freightos (NASDAQ:CRGO) announced on Tuesday preliminary Q1 transactions of 370.9K, vs. management's expectations of 362.0K - 370.0K and representing year-over-year growth of 25%.

Gross booking value is expected to be $276.1M, vs. management's expectations of $272.0M - $280.0M and representing year-over-year growth of 43%.

"While tariffs may affect the growth of world trade, there are still hundreds of billions of dollars of international freight services being booked offline, so a change in trade policies is expected to have only a marginal impact on the vast growth opportunity available for our digital platform," said CEO Zvi Schreiber.

The company said the financial results for the first quarter will be reported in the second half of May.

data.title: Albertsons targets $1.5B in productivity savings through 2027 with focus on digital growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Albertsons Companies, Inc. (ACI) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Vivek Sankaran highlighted his retirement and introduced Susan Morris as the new CEO. Morris emphasized her extensive tenure and strong alignment with the company’s strategic goals. Sankaran noted, "Within the few months since the termination of the merger, our mojo is back."
  • Susan Morris provided key updates, reporting Q4 identical sales growth of 2.3%, adjusted EBITDA of $855 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.46. Morris outlined strategic priorities, including accelerating digital platforms, enhancing customer loyalty programs, and driving productivity.
  • E-commerce grew 24% in Q4, with penetration exceeding 8% of grocery revenue. Top-performing markets surpassed 10%. The company plans to expand digital capabilities, particularly through its mobile app and loyalty integration.
  • Pharmacy revenue increased 18% YoY, driven by immunization growth and integrated health offerings. The company sees significant lifetime value from cross-shopping pharmacy and grocery customers.
  • CFO Sharon McCollam detailed productivity initiatives expected to yield $1.5 billion in savings through 2027, with reinvestment into growth areas like digital and pharmacy.

Outlook

  • Management provided fiscal 2025 guidance, projecting ID sales growth between 1.5% and 2.5%, adjusted EBITDA of $3.8 billion to $3.9 billion, and adjusted EPS in the range of $2.03 to $2.16.
  • Inflation is expected to range from 1.5% to 2%. Adjusted EBITDA includes approximately $65 million from a 53rd week.
  • Capital expenditures are anticipated to be between $1.7 billion and $1.9 billion, focusing on store modernization and digital investments.
  • McCollam indicated, "We will be making accelerated investments in digital growth, Albertsons Media Collective, and health and pharmacy."

Financial Results

  • Q4 gross margin was 27.4%; excluding fuel and LIFO expense, gross margin decreased by 45 basis points due to pharmacy sales and digital volume growth, partially offset by productivity gains.
  • Selling and administrative expense rate was 25.7%, with a five basis point improvement excluding fuel. This was driven by lower merger-related costs and employee cost efficiencies.
  • Interest expenses reduced to $101.5 million due to lower outstanding debt.
  • Capital expenditures totaled $485 million, focusing on digital and store fleet modernization.

Q&A

  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Asked about pricing investments given the consumer environment. Susan Morris explained a "surgical approach" to price adjustments by category and market, emphasizing the use of advanced tools to optimize elasticity.
  • Mark Carden, UBS: Inquired about demand growth for Albertsons Media Collective. Morris noted it is still nascent but outpacing market growth and highlighted plans to expand digital inventory and partnerships.
  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo: Asked about FY25 investment timing. McCollam explained that investments in digital and pharmacy would be front-loaded, with returns expected in the latter half of the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on pricing strategies and investment timing. Concerns over macroeconomic uncertainty and consumer behavior were evident in their questions.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing progress in digital engagement, productivity, and cost control. Statements like "Our Customers for Life strategy is working" reinforced their positive sentiment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 EPS of $0.46 compared to $0.71 in Q3, reflecting increased investment and seasonal shifts.
  • Identical sales growth improved slightly from 2% in Q3 to 2.3% in Q4, driven by pharmacy and digital sales.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize long-term investments and productivity, as opposed to short-term margin expansion.
  • Analyst focus transitioned from merger impacts in Q3 to strategic execution in Q4, highlighting a shift in priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged inflationary pressures and the need for strategic pricing adjustments to maintain competitiveness.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential gross margin pressures from digital and pharmacy mix shifts.
  • The company faces risks from macroeconomic uncertainty and evolving consumer spending patterns.

Final Takeaway

Albertsons Companies demonstrated strong progress in digital engagement, loyalty expansion, and productivity improvements in Q4 2024. With a $1.5 billion productivity savings target through 2027, the company aims to reinvest in growth areas like e-commerce, pharmacy, and retail media. Fiscal 2025 guidance reflects continued confidence in its "Customers for Life" strategy, supported by a focus on technology and strategic pricing adjustments to navigate evolving market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: This is how Trump’s policies complicate economic forecasting – Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Economists are struggling with President Donald Trump's boleo of escalation and de-escalation of erratic tariff policies, according to Morgan Stanley.

The investment bank said these sudden policy shifts, particularly those targeting China, make calculating trade elasticities “near impossible” while increasing recession risks as businesses delay critical investments.

“Greater policy uncertainty increases the risks of a business shutdown,” said Seth B. Carpenter, chief global economist at Morgan Stanley.

In a note, Morgan Stanley analysts noted that a complex dual tariff structure further complicates predictions, with sector-specific measures under Sections 201, 232, and 301 overlapping broader country-level tariffs authorized by IEEPA.

Analysts said that sector-level tariffs may take precedence due to their different legal frameworks, creating what they termed a “predicament for businesses.”

“Clear as mud?” Carpenter quipped, referring to situations where goods exempted from reciprocal tariffs could suddenly face Section 232 duties if investigations conclude within the mandated 360-day timeframe.

In addition, the interaction between different tariff types could result in lower effective rates for some exporters while exposing others to higher costs, according to the investment bank.

Analysts emphasized that the final tariff allocations — crucial for accurate growth and inflation forecasts — will only be measurable as they unfold in real time.

“Defining the final adjustment…will only be possible in real time,” Carpenter said, adding that fiscal, immigration, and other policies further compound the already significant uncertainty.

Finding market equilibrium has become increasingly difficult. “We are all looking for profits, but sometimes there may be none to be had,” Carpenter said, concluding that attempts to isolate tariff impacts amid overlapping policy collisions would be futile, leaving both economists and markets bracing for continued surprises.

More on markets:

  • Apollo critiques 60/40 portfolio as returns fall short since 2022
  • Will The Plunging Dollar Lead To A Recession And Bear Market
  • Air travel slumps as Apollo points to a noticeable decline in airports traffic
  • Trump’s 90-day tariff pause is not as helpful as it sounds, Citi says
  • Flight to safety: 10 dividend stocks with strong SA quant grades amid market volatility

data.title: Peabody Energy delays private debt refinancing of $2.1B loan - Bloomberg
data.description:
Peabody Energy (NYSE:BTU) has held off seeking a $500M private debt, which would help refinance an acquisition loan after the planned deal was placed under review, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

Financial firm Jefferies earlier this year was marketing the new loan - which would partially refinance a $2.1B bridge facility backing Peabody’s (NYSE:BTU) takeover of Anglo American's steelmaking coal business - to regional private credit firms.

The company had planned to refinance the bridge loan before the acquisition was scheduled to close in this year's H1, and was planning an $800M bond to refinance the remaining loan, according to the report.

Peabody (BTU) said last week it is reviewing the deal worth up to $3.78B to buy Anglo's coal portfolio after workers at Queensland's Moranbah North Mine had to be evacuated due to a fire; Anglo said both parties remain in talks.

data.title: Excellon announces private placement for proceeds of up to C$5M
data.description:
• Excellon Resources (TSX:EXN:CA) said on Tuesday that it has entered into an agreement with Red Cloud Securities in connection with a best efforts private placement for aggregate gross proceeds of up to C$5M from the sale of up to 47.62M units of the company at a price of C$0.105 per unit. 
  • Eric Sprott has made a lead order to subscribe for units under the offering.
  • Each unit will consist of one common share of the company and one-half of one common share purchase warrant. Each warrant shall entitle the holder to purchase one common share at a price of C$0.15 per warrant share. 
  • The agents will have an option to sell up to an additional 7.14M units at the offering price for up to an additional C$750,000 in gross proceeds.
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds of the offering for advancing development of the Mallay Mine, working capital and general corporate purposes.

data.title: Sanofi flunks mid-stage trial for asthma drug
data.description:
Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) announced Tuesday that its TIDE-Asthma Phase 2 study for its T-cell-depleting monoclonal antibody amlitelimab didn’t reach the primary endpoint for the highest dose level but indicated nominal significance at the medium dose.

Citing early results from the placebo-controlled trial, which enrolled over 400 adults with moderate-to-severe asthma, the company said amlitelimab reduced asthma exacerbations at the high dose at week 60 with a numerically superior impact.

Additionally, there were significant and clinically meaningful reductions in asthma exacerbations at the medium dose level over the same period.

“These results demonstrate that amlitelimab has potential to improve key disease outcomes in asthma patients with continued unmet need," the French drugmaker said, noting that plans are underway for a Phase 3 program for the drug.

“If the preliminary effect we have seen is confirmed in phase 3 studies, amlitelimab could become a differentiated treatment option in asthma,” Sanofi’s (NASDAQ:GCVRZ) R&D head, Houman Ashrafian, added.

data.title: Nvidia, Broadcom, Marvell among Oppenheimer's top chip picks ahead of earnings season
data.description:
Ahead of earnings season, Oppenheimer reiterated that Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO), Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) and Monolithic Power Systems (NASDAQ:MPWR) are its top picks in the semiconductor space.

“Against a noisy macro/tariff backdrop, we view AI as the best/safest growth vector,” analyst Rick Schafer wrote in a note to clients. “Upside results likely for most as companies benefit from tariff-induced pull-ins. We expect [management] outlooks to be tempered by trade war uncertainties and general lack of visibility. We see limited direct impact to AI spend at least near term.”

Delving deeper, Schafer said the expectations are that Nvidia will sell the equivalent of around 40,000 NVL72 units in the quarter, despite a “slower” start to the period, as cloud service provider capex is still expected to rise 40% in 2025.

And even though semiconductors are (mostly) exempt from the Trump administration's tariffs, there is talk of industry-specific tariffs still coming down the pipe, which could have negative implications.

“We see tariff-related price hikes most acutely impacting end-demand for consumer products like PCs/ smartphones,” Schafer added.

In conjunction with the recent market volatility, Schafer lowered his price targets on Analog Devices (ADI), Marvell, Monolithic Power, NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) and Veeco Instruments (VECO) to “reflect multiple compression.”

data.title: Lyell Immunopharma gets FDA RMAT status for lymphoma treatment
data.description:
• Lyell Immunopharma (NASDAQ:LYEL) said on Tuesday that the U.S. FDA granted Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy status to LYL314 for the treatment of large B-cell lymphoma.
  • Stock rose about 9% in morning trade.
  • The RMAT designation was granted based on data from the ongoing early-stage trial of LYL314 in patients with relapsed and/or refractory large B-cell lymphoma which had an overall response rate of 94%, while 71% of patients achieved a complete response by three months.
  • Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy designation entails benefits such as early interactions with the FDA to discuss surrogate or intermediate endpoints that could support the accelerated approval and meet post-approval requirements.

data.title: Short sellers boost bets against S&P 500 industrial stocks for the second month
data.description:
Short sellers boosted their bets against the S&P 500 industrial sector stocks in March, with the S&P 500 Industrial ETF (NYSEARCA: XLI) down for the second month, losing almost 3.9%.

As of March's end, the average short interest rate in S&P 500 industrials rose to 2.15%, up 4 bps from 2.11% at the end of February.

The S&P 500's industrial sector (NYSEARCA: XLI) lost 3.9% in March, compared to the broader S&P 500, which was down 5.9%.

Short interest, which could be an indicator of pessimism, calculates the number of shares sold against the company float. (Short Interest % = Number of Shares Sold Short ÷ Stock Float).

Most shorted stocks:

United Airlines Holding (UAL) - 5.95% vs 4.09% last month.

Southwest Airlines (LUV) - 5.84% vs. 5.50%.

Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) - 5.08% vs. 4.33%.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) - 4.92% VS 5.09%.

Dayforce (DAY) - 4.88% VS 5.62%.

Least shorted stocks:

RTX Corporation (RTX) - 0.69% vs 0.83% last month.

Waste Management (WM) - 0.94% vs 0.93%.

Parker-Hannifin (PH) - 0.99% vs 0.88%.

AMETEK (AME) - 0.99% vs 0.97%.

Broadridge Financial Solutions (BR) - 1.00% vs 0.89%.

Within the industry, Passenger Airlines remained the most shorted industry within the industrial sector, with 5.03% short interest at the end of March, compared to 5.03% last month. Software were the second most shorted industry with a short interest of 5.00% compared to 5.38% at the end of February. Environmental and Facilities Services is the least shorted sector with short interest of 1.30% at the end of March.
